title: Learning to Parse: Prosody, Weight, and the Sausage Machine Revisited
subtitle: J.D. Fodor's 1998 revision of parsing theory

children.children: Here's a question that split linguistics for decades: does your brain come PRE-WIRED to parse sentences, or does it LEARN how to parse from experience?

The nativists said: parsing is innate. Universal Grammar gives you the machinery. The empiricists said: parsing is learned. You hear millions of sentences and figure out the patterns. They argued past each other for years.
In 1998, Janet Dean Fodor proposed a surprising answer: BOTH. But not in the boring compromise way. Her insight was genuinely weird and genuinely brilliant.

Fodor's claim: the parsing MECHANISM is innate — you're born with it. But it needs to be CALIBRATED to the specific language you grow up speaking. The calibration IS the learning. The mechanism is universal; the tuning is particular.

props.analogy: A guitar comes with strings — that's innate. But the strings need to be TUNED to a specific key before they produce music. You don't learn to have strings. You learn the tuning. A guitar tuned for flamenco and one tuned for blues have the same hardware but sound completely different. The tuning IS the learning.
props.insight: Your parsing mechanism is the guitar. English, Japanese, and Turkish are different tunings. The strings are universal; the music is language-specific. Fodor's question isn't 'do you learn to parse?' but 'what exactly gets tuned?'

children.children: This reframing changed everything. Instead of asking 'is parsing innate or learned?' Fodor asked: what are the TUNABLE PARAMETERS of the parsing mechanism? What knobs does experience turn?

Her answer involved three radical ideas: the Antigravity Law, the Same-Size-Sister principle, and a complete revision of how the Sausage Machine's PPP works. Each one depended on something nobody had taken seriously before in parsing theory — the SOUND of the sentence in your head.

props.title: What Needs Tuning?
props.prompt: Consider these parsing differences across languages. What parameter could explain each one?

scenarios.input: English: 'the man with the telescope' → PP attaches LOW (to 'man'). Spanish: equivalent construction → PP attaches HIGH (to the verb).
scenarios.reveal: Same structure, different default attachment. The parsing mechanism handles PPs in both languages — but the DEFAULT DIRECTION of attachment differs. This is a tunable parameter: how strongly does the parser prefer low vs. high attachment? English tunes it one way, Spanish another.

scenarios.input: Japanese is head-final (verb at end). English is head-initial (verb near start). Both are parsed incrementally.
scenarios.reveal: The parser must handle words arriving in different orders. It doesn't need different MACHINERY for each — it needs different EXPECTATIONS about where heads will appear. The head-direction parameter is one of the 'guitar strings' that gets tuned by exposure.

scenarios.input: Why can't this just be learned from scratch?
scenarios.reveal: Children parse sentences BEFORE they have enough data to learn parsing from statistics alone. A 2-year-old handles complex syntax with very limited input. The mechanism must be largely in place already — what changes is the calibration. Just as a guitar doesn't learn to vibrate, but it does need to be tuned.

children.children: Remember the Sausage Machine's two key principles? Late Closure says: attach incoming material LOW — to the most recent phrase. Minimal Attachment says: attach with the fewest new nodes — which often means attaching HIGH. These two principles often CONFLICT.

Late Closure pulls things DOWN the tree (attach locally, to the nearest phrase). Minimal Attachment pulls things UP (attach simply, with fewer nodes, often to a higher phrase). For decades, the field debated which one wins when they clash.
Fodor noticed something nobody else had: the WINNER depends on the LENGTH of the constituent being attached.

Fodor's Antigravity Law: HEAVY constituents resist Late Closure. The longer a phrase is, the more it 'floats up' the tree — defying the gravitational pull of local attachment. Short phrases sink low. Long phrases rise high.
Read this: 'I saw the man with the telescope.'
Where does 'with the telescope' attach? Most people read it as modifying 'the man' — the man HAS the telescope. Low attachment. Late Closure wins.
Now read this: 'I saw the man with the large brass antique telescope from the Victorian era.'

Suddenly that PP doesn't feel like it's modifying 'man' anymore. It feels like it's modifying 'saw' — I used this elaborate telescope to see the man. The SAME syntactic structure, but the heavy PP has FLOATED UP the tree. Antigravity.

props.title: The Antigravity Effect
props.prompt: For each pair, notice how increasing constituent weight changes where the PP attaches. Does it modify the noun (LOW) or the verb (HIGH)?

scenarios.input: SHORT: 'She called the student with good grades.'
scenarios.reveal: LOW attachment: the student HAS good grades. 'With good grades' modifies 'the student.' The PP is short (3 words), so Late Closure wins — it sinks to the nearest noun phrase. Gravity pulls it down.

scenarios.input: LONG: 'She called the student with remarkably good grades in advanced mathematics and theoretical physics from the prestigious university.'
scenarios.reveal: HIGH attachment creeps in: it starts to feel like she called the student BY MEANS OF this elaborate credential — or at minimum, the sentence feels harder to parse with low attachment. The PP is now massive (15+ words). It resists Late Closure. It floats up. Antigravity.

scenarios.input: SHORT: 'The spy watched the man with binoculars.'
scenarios.reveal: Ambiguous, but LOW attachment is preferred: the man has binoculars. Short PP = Late Closure wins = low attachment.

scenarios.input: LONG: 'The spy watched the man with the high-powered military-grade binoculars equipped with infrared night vision and laser range-finding capability.'
scenarios.reveal: HIGH attachment dominates: the spy is USING the binoculars to watch. The PP is so heavy it has broken free from the noun phrase and floated up to attach to the verb. The same binoculars, the same syntax — but the weight changed everything.

This is the puzzle Fodor set out to solve. WHY should the length of a phrase change where it attaches? Length isn't a syntactic property — grammar doesn't care if a phrase is 3 words or 30. Something OUTSIDE the grammar is driving this. That something, Fodor argued, is prosody — the rhythm and melody of the sentence in your head. And that insight led her to the Same-Size-Sister principle.

children.children: Think of the guitar again. The Antigravity Law is like discovering that thick strings (heavy constituents) naturally vibrate at a lower frequency and RESIST being tuned to high notes. The physical properties of the string constrain the tuning. Similarly, the 'weight' of a constituent constrains where the parser can comfortably attach it.
Here's where Fodor pulled everything together into a single, elegant principle.
She noticed that the Antigravity Law wasn't the whole story. It wasn't just that heavy things float up. It was something deeper: constituents prefer to be attached next to SISTERS OF SIMILAR SIZE.

The Same-Size-Sister Principle: a constituent prefers to attach at a position in the tree where its SISTER constituent is roughly the same size. Light + Light = natural. Heavy + Heavy = natural. Light + Heavy = awkward.
This single principle explains BOTH Late Closure AND the Antigravity Law. Watch:
Late Closure explained: short phrases attach LOW because down in the tree, the other constituents are also small. A short PP attaches to a nearby NP because they're the SAME SIZE. Same-size sisters.

Antigravity explained: long phrases float UP because up in the tree, the other constituents are also large (whole VPs, whole clauses). A heavy PP attaches high because up there, it finds sisters of matching size. It's not 'defying gravity' — it's SEEKING ITS OWN KIND.

props.analogy: Think of a dinner table. You naturally seat people in balanced pairs: two kids together, two adults together. You wouldn't seat a toddler next to a linebacker — the mismatch feels wrong. Syntactic trees work the same way. Constituents want to sit next to siblings of similar stature.
props.insight: Same-size-sister isn't a rule the parser follows — it's a consequence of how prosodic packaging works. Balanced packages parse smoothly. Unbalanced ones create processing difficulty. The parser doesn't 'prefer' balance — balance is just easier to process.

children.children: Read these aloud (or 'hear' them in your head):
BALANCED: 'She gave [the man] [the book].' — Both short. Both NPs. Feels natural and rhythmic.

BALANCED: 'She gave [the tall distinguished gentleman from the embassy] [the thick classified dossier with the red stamp].' — Both long. Both heavy NPs. Still feels balanced, even though it's complex.
UNBALANCED: 'She gave [the book] [to the tall distinguished gentleman from the embassy who had arrived that morning in a black diplomatic car].' — Short + very long. Feels lopsided. Awkward.
UNBALANCED: 'She gave [the tall distinguished gentleman from the embassy who had arrived that morning in a black diplomatic car] [it].' — Very long + tiny. Even more awkward.

props.title: Same-Size-Sister Intuition Test
props.prompt: For each pair, judge which version feels more natural. Then see if Same-Size-Sister predicted your preference.

scenarios.input: A: 'I put the book on the table.' vs. B: 'I put the book on the large antique mahogany table that my grandmother brought from Italy.'
scenarios.reveal: A feels more natural. In A, both [the book] and [on the table] are short — same-size sisters. In B, the PP is massive while 'the book' is tiny — size mismatch. Same-Size-Sister predicts A is easier. You'd improve B by making 'the book' heavier: 'I put the rare first-edition book on the large antique mahogany table...'

scenarios.input: A: 'The fact that she left surprised him.' vs. B: 'The fact that she left early that morning without saying goodbye to anyone surprised him.'
scenarios.reveal: A feels more natural because [the fact that she left] and [surprised him] are roughly balanced. In B, the subject is enormous while the predicate is tiny — a lopsided tree. Same-Size-Sister predicts B is harder. It's like seating an elephant next to a mouse.

scenarios.input: A: 'She said she would leave.' vs. B: 'She said that in light of the recent developments and the ongoing investigation she would seriously consider the possibility of leaving.'
scenarios.reveal: A is balanced: short matrix clause, short complement. B has a massive complement dwarfing the tiny matrix clause 'She said.' Same-Size-Sister correctly predicts that B feels heavier and harder. But notice: B isn't ungrammatical — it's just uncomfortable. That discomfort IS the same-size-sister violation.

Same-Size-Sister unifies everything. Late Closure? Short things attach low because that's where they find same-size sisters. Antigravity? Long things float up because that's where THEY find same-size sisters. It's not two competing principles — it's ONE principle that produces different effects depending on constituent weight. This is the revelation. One guitar string, tuned differently for light and heavy phrases.

children.children: But WHY does constituent weight matter? Grammar doesn't know how many words are in a phrase. Syntax is blind to length. So what mechanism could possibly make the parser sensitive to it?
This is where Fodor's deepest insight lives. She went back to the Sausage Machine — specifically, to the PPP, the Preliminary Phrase Packager — and REVISED it.
The original PPP (Frazier & Fodor 1978) had a window of about 6 WORDS. Fodor's revision said: the window isn't measured in words. It's measured in PROSODY.

Fodor's Revised PPP: when you read a sentence — even SILENTLY — you assign an implicit prosodic contour to it. You 'hear' the rhythm, the stress, the phrasing in your mind's ear. These prosodic contours DEFINE the packages that the PPP creates. The PPP's window is a PROSODIC PHRASE, not a word count.

props.analogy: Reading is like humming. Even when you read silently, you assign a melody to the sentence — rises, falls, pauses, emphasis. Try it now: read 'The PRESIDENT of the United States will ADDRESS the NATION tonight' silently. Did you hear the stress pattern? The pauses? You're humming the sentence in your head. That humming determines how you parse it.
props.insight: The melody you assign to a sentence ISN'T just decoration. It DETERMINES how the PPP packages the words. Different melodies → different packages → different parses. This is why the same words, read with different prosody, can mean different things.

children.children: Now the Antigravity Law and Same-Size-Sister suddenly make MECHANICAL sense:

A SHORT constituent fits inside an existing prosodic phrase. It gets packaged WITH its neighbor — and that neighbor becomes its sister. Low attachment. Same-size sisters, because they share a prosodic phrase.

A LONG constituent gets its OWN prosodic phrase. It becomes its own package. When a constituent is its own package, it can't attach low to the neighboring phrase — it's in a DIFFERENT package. So it attaches higher, where it finds another large constituent (another full package) as its sister.
'I saw the man | with the telescope.' — One prosodic phrase. 'With the telescope' is packaged with 'the man.' Low attachment. The man has the telescope.

'I saw the man | with the large brass antique telescope from the Victorian era.' — The PP is so long it gets its OWN prosodic phrase (notice the implicit pause or breath after 'man'). It's a separate package. It can't attach inside 'the man' because it's not IN that package. It attaches higher — to 'saw.' I used the telescope to see the man.
The prosodic break CREATES the attachment difference. Length triggers prosodic phrasing. Prosodic phrasing defines packages. Packages determine attachment. That's the causal chain.

props.title: Evidence: Prosody During Silent Reading
props.content: How do we know readers assign prosody silently? Bader (1998) provided striking evidence. He studied German sentences that required reanalysis (garden-path recovery). Some reanalyses required changing ONLY the syntactic structure. Others required changing BOTH syntax and the implicit prosodic contour. Result: reanalysis was significantly MORE costly when prosody also needed repair. If readers weren't assigning prosody during silent reading, both types of reanalysis should have been equally costly — only syntax was being revised. The extra cost of prosodic repair proves that readers HAD assigned prosodic structure, and that undoing it costs additional processing effort. Silent reading isn't silent at all. Your inner voice is doing real prosodic work, and that work has real consequences for parsing.

children.children: Back to the guitar: prosody is the RESONANCE of the string. When you pluck a guitar string, it doesn't just vibrate at one frequency — it produces overtones that depend on the string's length and tension. Similarly, when you read a sentence, it doesn't just have syntax — it has prosodic resonance that depends on constituent length and weight. The resonance determines how the pieces group together. Fodor showed that parsing isn't just about structure — it's about the MUSIC of language.

props.title: From Prosodic Packaging to Attention Patterns

children.children: Fodor's revised PPP — where prosodic phrases define processing packages — maps onto something we can actually observe inside transformer language models: attention patterns in the early layers.

When we look at what attention heads in layers 0–6 of GPT-2 actually DO, we see them grouping tokens into contiguous chunks — phrases, roughly. These chunks look suspiciously like prosodic phrases. They're not defined by strict syntactic boundaries. They're defined by patterns that correlate with prosodic phrasing: function words group with their content words, heavy phrases get segmented into sub-chunks, balanced constituents get parallel treatment.

props.title: Fodor → Transformer Mapping
props.prompt: Each Fodor concept has a corresponding mechanism in transformer models. Explore the parallels.

scenarios.input: PPP's prosodic packages → Early-layer attention chunks (L0–6)
scenarios.reveal: The PPP groups words into prosodic-phrase-sized packages. Early transformer layers group tokens into local attention clusters. Both are doing the same thing: creating manageable CHUNKS from raw input before attempting global structure. The chunk boundaries aren't random — they follow patterns that align with prosodic phrasing.

Transformers show a preference for balanced constituency in their internal representations. Attention heads that track syntactic structure tend to create BALANCED trees — sister nodes with similar span lengths receive more coherent attention patterns. Unbalanced structures (tiny node + massive node) produce more diffuse, less confident attention. This mirrors Fodor's Same-Size-Sister: balance isn't a rule, it's a processing preference.

scenarios.input: Antigravity Law → Length-dependent attachment in attention heads
scenarios.reveal: Long constituents in transformer input tend to form their own attention clusters (their own 'packages'), which then attach to higher-level structure. Short constituents get absorbed into neighboring clusters. This IS the Antigravity Law: heavy things get their own package and attach high; light things merge with neighbors and attach low. Gradient descent discovered Fodor's principle independently.

scenarios.input: L11 consensus breakdown → Detecting prosodic-syntactic mismatch
scenarios.reveal: When the prosodic packaging (early-layer chunking) doesn't match the actual syntactic structure, Layer 11's consensus neurons fire differently — consensus drops. This is exactly what Bader showed for humans: when prosody and syntax conflict, processing cost increases. L11 is detecting the same mismatch that makes garden-path recovery harder when prosody also needs repair.

scenarios.input: Calibration / parameter tuning → Fine-tuning and in-context learning
scenarios.reveal: Fodor's insight that the parser is innate but CALIBRATED maps to how transformers work: the architecture is fixed (innate), but the weights are tuned by training data (calibration). Different training data = different 'languages' = different tuning of the same mechanism. Even in-context learning (few-shot prompting) is a form of rapid calibration — temporarily tuning the parser's expectations for a specific task. The guitar strings are always there; the tuning changes.

children.children: The deep parallel: Fodor argued that the parser is an innate mechanism that learns by tuning parameters to a specific language. Transformers ARE innate mechanisms (fixed architecture) that learn by tuning parameters (weights) to specific data. Fodor's revised PPP — where prosodic packaging drives parsing — may describe not just how humans parse, but how ANY efficient sequential processor will learn to parse. The prosodic packaging strategy is a computational attractor.

questions.question: What is Fodor's answer to 'Is parsing learned or innate?'
options: Entirely innate — Universal Grammar handles everything
Entirely learned — statistical learning from input
The mechanism is innate but needs to be CALIBRATED to a specific language through experience
Some languages require innate parsing, others require learned parsing
questions.explanation: Fodor's key insight: the parsing mechanism is innate (like a guitar having strings), but it needs to be calibrated to a specific language (like tuning the strings to a key). The tuning IS the learning. The architecture is universal; the parameter settings are language-specific.

questions.question: What does the Antigravity Law predict?
options: All constituents prefer high attachment
Heavy constituents resist Late Closure and tend to attach HIGHER in the tree
Short constituents float up while long ones sink down
Minimal Attachment always beats Late Closure
questions.explanation: The Antigravity Law: heavy (long) constituents resist the pull of Late Closure (low, local attachment) and float UP the tree. Short constituents obey Late Closure and attach low. The WEIGHT of the constituent determines which principle wins.

questions.question: How does Same-Size-Sister unify Late Closure and the Antigravity Law?
options: It replaces both principles with a new one
Short things attach low (finding small sisters) and long things attach high (finding large sisters) — both are seeking same-size sisters
It proves that Late Closure is always correct
It shows that constituent weight doesn't actually matter
questions.explanation: Same-Size-Sister is the deeper principle beneath both. Late Closure = short constituents attaching low where they find other short sisters. Antigravity = long constituents floating up where they find other long sisters. Both are instances of the same preference: attach where your sister is YOUR SIZE.

questions.question: Why does constituent LENGTH affect parsing, according to Fodor's revised PPP?
options: Longer constituents are harder to remember
The grammar has length-sensitive rules
Long constituents get their own PROSODIC PHRASE, becoming separate packages that attach differently
The SSS can't handle long phrases
questions.explanation: Length triggers prosodic phrasing. A short PP fits inside an existing prosodic phrase (same package as its neighbor → low attachment). A long PP gets its own prosodic phrase (separate package → higher attachment). Prosody is the mechanism that translates length into attachment preference.

questions.question: What evidence supports the claim that readers assign prosody during SILENT reading?
options: People move their lips when they read
Bader (1998) showed that reanalysis is MORE costly when both prosody AND syntax need repair, proving prosody was assigned
Brain scans show auditory cortex activation during reading
People read faster when there are commas
questions.explanation: Bader (1998) compared reanalysis that required only syntactic repair vs. reanalysis requiring both syntactic AND prosodic repair. The extra cost of prosodic repair proves readers HAD assigned prosodic structure during silent reading — if they hadn't, both types should be equally costly.

children.children: • Fodor, J.D. (1998). 'Learning to Parse?' Journal of Psycholinguistic Research, 27(2), 285–319.
• Frazier, L. & Fodor, J.D. (1978). 'The Sausage Machine: A New Two-Stage Parsing Model.' Cognition, 6, 291–325.
• Bader, M. (1998). 'Prosodic Influences on Reading Syntactically Ambiguous Sentences.' — Evidence for implicit prosody during silent reading.
• Fodor, J.D. (2002). 'Prosodic Disambiguation in Silent Reading.' — Further development of the Implicit Prosody Hypothesis.
• Kimball, J. (1973). 'Seven Principles of Surface Structure Parsing.' — Background on parsing principles that Fodor's work revises.
